questions.prompt: Approximately what has been the long-run real annual return on US large-cap stocks (1926-2024)?
questions.explanation: Around 7-8% real per year. Nominal returns are closer to 10%; the difference is long-run inflation.

questions.prompt: Asset A: E[R]=12%, σ=30%. Asset B: E[R]=8%, σ=20%. Correlation 0.3. 50/50 portfolio expected return (%)?
questions.explanation: E[R_p] = 0.5×12 + 0.5×8 = 10%. The expected-return calculation is just a weighted average — diversification only helps risk, not expected return.

questions.prompt: Same two assets. 50/50 portfolio standard deviation (%)? Round to integer.
Why is idiosyncratic risk not priced (no expected return for bearing it) in CAPM-style models?
choices: Because investors can diversify it away for free by holding many stocks; only systematic risk earns a premium
Because it's too small to matter
questions.explanation: Idiosyncratic risk averages to ~0 in a diversified portfolio. Investors who fail to diversify bear it without compensation — that's their choice, not a market failure.
